title: User Guide
description: Comprehensive guide for Naflows' NASS users to get started with the platform.

vocabulary.definition: A service is an application or API that you want to protect using NASS. Each service is registered in NASS and can have multiple instances.
A client is an application that requests access to a service. Clients must be registered in NASS and can have different access levels.
An access token is a credential that a client uses to access a service. Tokens are issued by NASS and have a limited lifespan.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
In this section, we'll introduce the concept of services in NASS and explain their importance.

What is a Service?

A service in NASS represents an application or API that you want to protect. By registering your service with NASS, you can manage access control, monitor usage, and ensure secure communication between clients and your service.

Service Components

Each service consists of rights, tokens, and keys that enable your developers to work securely on your project without putting your service at risk. Rights can be customized and assigned from the Service Dashboard in your account.

Developer Keys

Every developer working on a service receives two keys:

  1. Private API Key – Allows the developer to authenticate requests made to the service while in development mode.
  2. Private Developer Key – Identifies actions performed by the developer to register logs, verify rights, and support secure project development.

Service Tokens

While keys are exclusively for developer access, tokens authenticate requests made by your service (the Client). The Service token is a secret known only to NASS and is randomly regenerated by the system. You can request a regeneration, but each service is subject to rate limitations.

Choosing a Plan

If you are not using the open-source version of NASS, you'll be prompted to select a plan when creating your service. Your plan determines the number of services you can create and the features available to you.

Plans are designed to accommodate different usage levels, from small projects to large-scale applications. Choose the plan that best suits your requirements and budget. If you're unsure which plan to select, contact our support team for assistance.

sections.title: Creating a Service
sections.content:
To create a service, first log in to your NASS account. Once logged in, navigate to the 'Services' section in the dashboard and click on 'Create New Service'.

Fill in the required details such as the service name, description, and select the appropriate plan if applicable. After submitting the form, your service will be created, and you'll be provided with the necessary keys and tokens to start integrating NASS with your application.

Service Details

When creating your service, make sure the following:

  • Service name is unique and descriptive.
  • Service DNS and IP adresses are correctly configured (the NASS is operating on production with these data in order to verify your service's identity).
  • All required fields are filled out.

Naflows offer different parameters to allow control over users' connections:

  • Display your service publicly on the Naflows Services Directory.
  • Allow or deny user' connection if not invited.

Service Requirements

Before creating a service, ensure that you have the following:

  • A valid Naflows account.
  • Basic understanding of your application's architecture and how it will interact with NASS.
  • Knowledge of the access control requirements for your service.

Post-Creation Steps

After creating your service, you can proceed to configure its settings, register clients, and start testing the integration. Refer to the subsequent sections in this guide for detailed instructions on these steps.

Please note that any service is subject to Naflows' moderation policies. All created services must comply with Naflows' Terms of Service and Privacy Policy and may be removed with or without notice if they violate these policies.

sections.title: Connecting Your Service
sections.content: Please read this guide for detailed instructions on implementing the connection with NASS' API.

vocabulary.definition: A mode that allows developers to test and connect their applications to the NASS API using developer credentials.
The live environment where the application is fully operational and accessible to end-users.
A unique key provided to each service that allows developers to authenticate requests made to the service while in development mode.
A key that identifies actions performed by the developer, used for logging, verifying rights, and supporting secure project development.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: This section provides an overview of how to connect your application to the NASS API.

sections.title: Connect in Development Mode
sections.content:
Instructions for connecting your application in development mode.

Connect your service

After creating a service, you can configure its settings such as access policies, rate limits, and allowed clients. Before starting to code tunnels and rights, you need to start the service on your Service Dashboard:

  • Go to the 'Services' section.
  • Select your service.
  • Click on 'Start Service' in the 'Overview' tab.

A success message should be displayed if the service starts successfully. Once started, you can begin implementing the necessary code to connect your application to the service.

Get your developer credentials

To connect your application to the service, you'll need your developer credentials. These include the Private API Key (unique for the service) and Private Developer Key provided when you created the service. Please note that each developer key is unique and private, and even the service's administrator cannot access a key that is not they own. You can find these keys in the 'Safety' tab of your Service Dashboard.

Make sure to keep these keys secure and do not expose them in your client-side code on production. Use them only in your server-side code to authenticate requests to NASS.

Implement the connection

To connect your application to the NASS API, you need to implement the connection logic in your code. Below is a basic example of how to do this using Node.js and the Axios library:

const axios = require('axios');
